stem: On the interval \( [0, 2\pi) \), which of the following is a solution of \( [[Ad]]\cos(2x) + ([[Bd]])\cos x + ([[Cd]]) = 0 \)? (Reduce it with a double-angle identity first.)
solution: Apply the double-angle identity \( \cos(2x) = 2\cos^2 x - 1 \); the equation becomes \( [[Ar]]\cos^2 x + ([[Br]])\cos x + ([[Cr]]) = 0 \), a quadratic in \( \cos x \) with rational roots. Solving \( \cos x = \) (a root) on \( [0, 2\pi) \) gives \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: The cosine of \( \tfrac{\pi}{6} \) is irrational, so it is not a root of the reduced quadratic.
The cosine of \( \tfrac{\pi}{4} \) is irrational, so this angle does not satisfy the equation.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{\pi}{2} \), landing on a non-solution.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{3\pi}{4} \), so its cosine is no longer a root.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{5\pi}{6} \), giving a non-solution.
The cosine of \( \tfrac{3\pi}{4} \) is irrational, so this angle is not a solution.

stem: On the interval \( [0, 2\pi) \), which of the following is a solution of \( [[Ap]]\sin^2 x + ([[Bp]])\cos x + ([[Cp]]) = 0 \)? (Reduce it with the Pythagorean identity first.)
solution: Apply the Pythagorean identity \( \sin^2 x = 1 - \cos^2 x \); the equation becomes \( [[Ar]]\cos^2 x + ([[Br]])\cos x + ([[Cr]]) = 0 \), a quadratic in \( \cos x \) with rational roots. Solving \( \cos x = \) (a root) on \( [0, 2\pi) \) gives \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: The cosine of \( \tfrac{\pi}{6} \) is irrational, so it is not a root of the reduced quadratic.
The cosine of \( \tfrac{\pi}{4} \) is irrational, so this angle does not satisfy the equation.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{\pi}{2} \), landing on a non-solution.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{3\pi}{4} \), so its cosine is no longer a root.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{5\pi}{6} \), giving a non-solution.
The cosine of \( \tfrac{3\pi}{4} \) is irrational, so this angle is not a solution.

stem: Find the value of \( x \) in \( \left(-\dfrac{\pi}{2}, \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right) \) satisfying \( [[A]]\sin x - ([[B]])\cos x = 0 \). (Divide by \( \cos x \) to reduce it to a single tangent identity first.)
solution: Dividing both sides by \( \cos x \) (valid since \( \cos x=0 \) is not a solution here) gives \( [[A]]\tan x = [[B]] \), so \( \tan x = \dfrac{[[B]]}{[[A]]} \) and \( x = \arctan\dfrac{[[B]]}{[[A]]} = [[answer]] \), which lies in \( \left(-\tfrac{\pi}{2},\tfrac{\pi}{2}\right) \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio inside the arctangent.
This has the wrong sign.
This drops the sign of \( B/A \) by taking absolute values.
This shifts the true solution by \( \tfrac{\pi}{2} \), leaving the principal branch.
This shifts the true solution by \( \tfrac{\pi}{4} \).
This reports the tangent ratio itself instead of the angle.

stem: On the interval \( [0, 2\pi) \), which of the following is a solution of \( [[m]]\sin(2x) - ([[B]])\sin x = 0 \)? (Use the double-angle identity for sine, then factor.)
solution: By the double-angle identity, \( \sin(2x) = 2\sin x\cos x \), so the equation factors as \( \sin x\,(2\cdot[[m]]\cos x - [[B]]) = 0 \). One branch gives \( \sin x = 0 \) and the other gives \( \cos x = \dfrac{[[B]]}{2\cdot[[m]]} \), a nice value; solving on \( [0,2\pi) \) gives (among others) \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: The cosine of \( \tfrac{\pi}{6} \) is irrational and does not satisfy either factor.
The cosine of \( \tfrac{\pi}{4} \) is irrational and does not satisfy either factor.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{\pi}{6} \), landing off both branches.
This shifts a true solution by \( \tfrac{\pi}{4} \), landing off both branches.
This is \( \tfrac{3\pi}{2} \), which satisfies neither \( \sin x=0 \) nor the cosine branch here.
This is \( \tfrac{\pi}{2} \), which satisfies neither factor for these coefficients.
